blocks.body: Gerund helps you talk about an action as an idea, activity, or process after certain verbs and prepositions.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"In this lesson you will see how the gerund works as the -ing form, when it behaves almost like a noun, and why a different pattern sounds unnatural after some words.","rules":[{"label":"THE -ING FORM","color":"emerald","text":"A gerund has the form verb + -ing: reading, swimming, studying.","example":"Reading before bed helps me relax."},{"label":"LIKE A NOUN","color":"blue","text":"A gerund can be the subject, the object, or the name of an activity.","example":"Swimming is good for you. / I enjoy swimming."},{"label":"AFTER VERBS AND PREPOSITIONS","color":"amber","text":"After many verbs and almost all prepositions, the natural choice is a gerund.","example":"avoid making / interested in learning"}]}

{"title":"1. Main form","intro":"A gerund looks like verb + -ing, but in a sentence it often works like a noun-like action or activity.","items":[{"label":"Subject","title":"Swimming is fun.","subtitle":"The gerund can stand at the beginning as the topic or activity."},{"label":"Object","title":"I enjoy reading.","subtitle":"After some verbs, the gerund comes after the main verb."},{"label":"After a preposition","title":"after leaving / good at cooking","subtitle":"After in, on, at, after, before, without, and other prepositions we use -ing."},{"label":"Name of an activity","title":"Cooking takes time.","subtitle":"The gerund often names a hobby, process, or general activity."}]}

{"title":"2. When to use the gerund","sections":[{"label":"As the subject or a general activity","color":"emerald","description":"Use a gerund when you talk about an activity in general or make it the topic of the sentence.","examples":[{"en":"Walking every day is good for your health.","ua":"Walking every day is good for your health."},{"en":"Learning English takes time.","ua":"Learning English takes time."},{"en":"Cooking at home saves money.","ua":"Cooking at home saves money."}]},{"label":"After certain verbs","color":"blue","description":"After verbs like enjoy, avoid, mind, finish, keep, the usual pattern is a gerund.","examples":[{"en":"She enjoys reading on the train.","ua":"She enjoys reading on the train."},{"en":"We finished writing the report at noon.","ua":"We finished writing the report at noon."},{"en":"Do you mind waiting a few minutes?","ua":"Do you mind waiting a few minutes?"}],"note":"Here the infinitive often sounds unnatural or is simply wrong."},{"label":"After prepositions","color":"amber","description":"After a preposition, we normally use the -ing form, even if another language prefers an infinitive.","examples":[{"en":"He left without saying goodbye.","ua":"He left without saying goodbye."},{"en":"I am interested in learning Spanish.","ua":"I am interested in learning Spanish."},{"en":"Before going out, check the lights.","ua":"Before going out, check the lights."}],"note":"After a preposition, do not use to learn or to go."}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Using a to-infinitive after a verb that takes a gerund.","wrong":"I enjoy to read before bed.","right":"✅ I enjoy reading before bed.","hint":"After enjoy, the natural pattern is the -ing form."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Leaving the bare verb as the subject.","wrong":"Swim is good for you.","right":"✅ Swimming is good for you.","hint":"When the action is the topic of the sentence, a gerund is often the right form."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Using to after a preposition.","wrong":"She is interested in to learn French.","right":"✅ She is interested in learning French.","hint":"After a preposition, use -ing, not to + verb."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["A gerund = verb + -ing used as an action, activity, or process.","The gerund often works as the subject, the object, or the form after a certain verb.","After prepositions, we almost always use -ing: after leaving, good at cooking.","After verbs like enjoy, avoid, mind, finish, keep, the gerund is usually the correct pattern.","If you are unsure after a preposition, -ing is almost always the safe choice."]}
